## [00:00 - 03:02]

By now you should have downloaded the template for the Fat Campaign Breakdown, which is found in your Google Analytics account under Customization and Custom Reports. So now if we click into it, I can just quickly show you how to read the data. So this ad account here, we are running a number of different campaigns and the structure of what's reported in Google Analytics matches with what we have created for our naming conventions in Ad Manager. So if I just filter out and only show SMC, just to keep it simple. So this is just a last 30 days report for this particular account. And what we can see here is for each of the different campaigns, I'm just looking at basic stats in Google Analytics. So I'm looking at sessions, revenue, equal mass conversion rate, average order value and the number of transactions. There's just five metrics that I've chosen to show in this view. So I'm looking at sales data here. There's also funnel data. If your funnel is set up properly, then you can see funnel data from Google Analytics. And this is specifically for data from Facebook ads. And what you can do is compare this against Facebook, especially where data is being lost. And if I go into, and then there's also session data here, session data, I've just included just as an FYI, there's not really much depth that you can go into here. We can see number of new sessions bounce rate. If search has been set up for Google Analytics, you can see that and duration length. This is kind of going a bit deeper into analytics. I'm not necessarily going to cover this into too much depth. But if you want, you know, you can draw down and have a look at that. Now if I go to just quickly show you the setup, which you can do in your own time as well, it's really, really simple. So here you can create multiple tabs if you want different views. And then within that you can group different metrics. So for example, I might create a new tab and I want to map overlay or a different type of table. I can do that. And you can drag and drop these around as you like. And you can add and remove as you like as well. So this is not fixed. Once you've imported this, you can play around with it and you can customize it. Don't worry if you make a mistake, you can re-import it, you can change the name, etc. But this is really important because this matches how I see data in ads manager. So campaign, ad set and ad. And these are the UTN parameters that we're pushing through. This has already been set. Now if you want, you can change the order and say, when I click a campaign, I want to see the ad or, you know, however you want to view that, maybe you want to just get rid of campaign and just have all your ad sets there. That's absolutely fine as well. And then this is where the filtering occurs on source and medium to just show traffic that has been tagged as Facebook and CPC.

## [03:02 - 06:02]

So that's the basic view of how the report's been set up. You don't need to worry too much about that. Once you get more experienced with the kind of set ups, you can customize it yourself. And also if you're using a UTM tracking that's different to what I use, then clearly that report won't work. And you might want to try some customizations, but that's slightly outside of the remit of this particular training. So the first part is once you've set up your UTM tracking in Google Analytics in Facebook, as per the training, then your data will show correctly. So for example, if I look here last 30 days, this particular campaign has generated 15,000 sessions. And by the way, sessions and visits are not always the same and visitors are unique. So I'm looking here, if I look at my sales data, I can see what revenue has brought and been brought in as last click data. That means that whatever Google Analytics sees as being the last click will get recorded. So what this tells me is my phase phase three prospecting campaign here has generated $23,000 when it was the last click. And I'll be showing you the different models in a separate video. But the cool thing here is I can actually drill down. Now there's many other things I can add and analyze and things like that that go kind of beyond the scope of this video. But I can drill down and I can see what's happening at the ad set level. And then I can drill down further and have a look at what's happening at the ad level as well. And what's crucial about this, as I'll show you in a separate video, is being able to compare this against what Facebook's seeing. And there's two scenarios here. One is that Facebook is not seeing the data, but Google Analytics is, which is interesting because that tells me there's something wrong with the Facebook side of things when it comes to tracking. Could be users opted out of tracking. Could be that there's a pixel problem. Maybe the pixel has been blocked. There's many scenarios where Facebook will be lower. Then there's scenarios where Facebook will be showing higher numbers, higher revenue, higher conversion rate than what's in Google Analytics. That's also not a bad thing because that's telling me Facebook thinks that it was the last click. But Google Analytics has seen something else as the last click. And in both scenarios, I want that data so that I can make decisions off the back of it as well. So just bear that in mind. As you're going through this process of getting the data, seeing the data come through, there are different things that we will be analyzing here as well. So take this, for example, we have an ad set with two ads running. And if I compare the conversion rates here, and we'll show this in a separate video, I want to compare that to what I'm seeing in ads manager, because it could well be that in ads manager, I'm just seeing complete different data as well.

## [06:02 - 06:51]

And if you want to then compare iOS versus Android, data that you can't see in Facebook at the breakdown level, hey, you can see it in Google Analytics just by using segments. This is one of the cool parts of getting your Google Analytics data set up and using a report like this. And this has been specifically set up in the way I've used it. This is something I've used for many, many years, I think since 2014. So back then we were using exactly the same process, but we didn't really have to rely on it as much because Facebook gave us all the data, right? And now with the privacy changes, we're kind of going back to our roots. So I've been doing this for a really long time. I'm hoping that as you go through this, you'll be able to pull out the gems yourself and really make better decisions on your media buying as well.
